scenario: Forge Bank stores 60000 customers per day in Camunda 8.8 inside a deposit-onboarding process. A modeler is choosing variable names that comply with Camunda 8.8 rules so that FEEL expressions in gateways and input mappings can read them safely across 250 instances per minute during peak hours of the onboarding flow.
question: Which proposed variable name complies with the Camunda 8.8 variable naming rules?

options.text: The name 1stOrder, intended as a short alphanumeric identifier mapping a customer's first order in the JSON payload of the instance.
The name customer_id, a snake_case alphanumeric identifier under 492 characters that does not collide with a FEEL keyword or operator.
The name order-number, a kebab-case identifier under 492 characters that maps to an order field in the JSON payload of the instance.
The name order number, a whitespace-separated alphanumeric identifier under 492 characters that maps to a JSON property of the instance.

a.text: Incorrect. A variable name may not start with a number, so 1stOrder is rejected by the documented rules; the suggested alternative is to lead with a letter, for example firstOrder, to satisfy the naming restrictions.

b.text: Correct. customer_id is an alphanumeric identifier using the underscore symbol in snake_case, stays well below the 492-character limit, and does not collide with any FEEL literal, keyword, or operator listed in the variable naming rules.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly disallows kebab-case names because the dash is parsed as the subtraction operator, so order-number cannot be used as a variable name.

d.text: Incorrect. Variable names may not contain whitespace, so order number is rejected; the suggested alternative is to use orderNumber or order_number to satisfy the naming restrictions.

explanation: Camunda 8 variable names are alphanumeric plus underscore, must not start with a number, must not contain whitespace, must not collide with FEEL literals, keywords, or operators, and must stay under 492 UTF-8 characters. camelCase and snake_case styles are recommended; kebab-case is rejected because the dash is an operator.
